column1: # Buffer-compatible format. Import directly. Schedule 3-5 posts per account per day.
# Spread across 4-5 days per account = 20 posts over ~5 days per niche.

column3: text
I run 20+ AI agents in parallel while I sleep. they scrape leads, generate content, analyze competitors, and build apps. woke up to 1,661 new leads in a CSV. the overnight shift doesn't need coffee.
replaced $347/mo in SaaS tools with python scripts over one weekend. zapier to n8n. buffer to github actions. ahrefs to serpapi. $23/mo total now. that's $3,888/year back.
500 cold emails. 30 days. 4.4% reply rate. 2 clients at $1,500 each. best template was 4 sentences. worst was 3 paragraphs. nobody reads long cold emails. nobody.
built a prayer app in 6 days. 55KB total. works offline. costs $0/mo to host. native app quotes were $12-18K. the PWA advantage is real and most devs are sleeping on it.
the gap between planning and revenue is exactly one cold email. i know because i spent 15 days planning and made $0. then i sent 500 emails and made $3,000.
my quant terminal tracks 150+ money methods across 12 spreadsheets. bloomberg for solopreneurs. if a method drops below 3% conversion it gets flagged automatically.
3 AI workflows. $20/month. 11 hours saved per week. competitor research in 90 seconds. meeting notes in 45 seconds. weekly reports in 90 seconds. the prompts are specific. that's why they work.
month 1 P&L. revenue: $3,147.72. expenses: $81. net: $3,066.72. 97% from cold outreach. everything else is compounding. month 2 is about distribution.
stop building 3 apps. build 1. ship it. get users. i built 3 PWAs in one week. only the one i actually promoted got 1,821 installs. the other two sat there. focus wins.
60% of my cold email replies came from follow-up 2 or 3. not the first email. most people send one email, get no response, and quit. the follow-up IS the strategy.
i priced my first notion template at $7. should have been $19. a template that saves someone 10+ hours is worth more than a chipotle bowl. underpricing is a fear response.
analyzed 200 landing pages. top 10% all had: 6-10 word headlines, 3 value bullets max, social proof above the fold, product preview visible, one CTA repeated 3 times.
my content distribution script is 87 lines of python. reads a CSV. posts to twitter and linkedin on a cron schedule via github actions. total cost: $0. forever.
10 products on gumroad. 6 PWA apps deployed. 4 newsletters running. 16 cron jobs. 80+ scripts. $81 in total expenses. the infrastructure is built. now it compounds. full breakdown in newsletter.
the overnight automation runs 30+ scripts at 2 AM. scrapes leads. checks competitor pricing. generates content. screens alpha. by 8 AM there's a prioritized TODO waiting for me.
if your landing page has more than one CTA you're losing conversions. every additional field reduces signups by 10-25%. one button. one action. make it obvious.
freelance arbitrage math: client pays $300 for a landing page. claude code builds it in 15 minutes. I QA for 5 minutes. deliver early. get 5-star review. margin: 95%+.
47 newsletter subscribers in week 1. not impressive. but each subscriber enters a 14-day sequence that sells a $12 product. 10% conversion = $4.70 per subscriber in LTV. math works at scale.
day 1: choose tech stack (4 hours). day 2-30: build. what i should have done: day 1: choose tech stack (20 minutes). day 1: start building. infrastructure is procrastination with a nice name.
the best program is the one you actually do. the best product is the one you actually ship. the best strategy is the one you actually execute. start messy. fix later.
the most honest prayer i've prayed in years was 6 words. 'God, I don't know how to talk to you anymore.' no structure. no theology. just honesty. that's enough.
surveyed 500+ people about prayer. 64% said 'I get distracted within 30 seconds.' the problem isn't discipline. it's that we're trying to pray for 30 minutes when 3 minutes actually sticks.
users who started with 3 minutes of prayer per day had 52% retention at 90 days. users who started with 7 minutes: 31%. consistency beats intensity. every single time.
Ramadan starts February 28. 16 days away. if you haven't started preparing your heart, today is the day. not tomorrow. not next week. right now.
early versions of the app tracked missed prayer days prominently. terrible idea. users who saw '3 days missed' felt worse, not motivated. replaced it with 'current streak.' focus on what you did.
morning prayer works better than evening prayer. 3.2x longer streaks for people who pray between 5-8 AM vs after 6 PM. evening prayer competes with exhaustion. morning prayer sets the tone.
gratitude is more powerful when it's specific. not 'thank you for this day.' try 'thank you that my kid laughed at breakfast.' specificity turns routine into real conversation.
Ramadan prep tip: start adjusting your sleep schedule now. shift bedtime 15 minutes earlier each night. by February 28 you'll be ready for suhoor without the shock.
78% of users who fell off their prayer streak restarted at least once. falling off is normal. restarting is the whole point. the app doesn't judge. neither does God.
built a prayer app that locks your phone screen until you engage with a short devotional. 55KB. works offline. the phone that distracts you becomes the thing that reminds you to pray.
Saturday prayer completions drop 34% compared to weekdays. weekends are the weak point. if you only pick one day to be intentional about prayer this week, make it Saturday.
the 4-part prayer framework that actually sticks: gratitude (2 min), confession (1 min), intercession (2 min), listening (2 min). 7 minutes total. or just the first two in 3 minutes.
social accountability for prayer works. users in our Telegram community had 2.1x longer prayer streaks than solo users. find one person. text each other 'prayed today.' that's enough.
the faith audience is generous but allergic to feeling sold to. one-time purchases with genuine value work better than subscriptions. $7 prayer journal outsells $3/month subscription 4 to 1.
15 days to Ramadan. download a prayer tracking app. set one goal: pray fajr on time for the first 10 days. that's it. one prayer. one habit. build from there.
guilt is the enemy of consistency. if you missed yesterday, the worst thing you can do is beat yourself up about it. the best thing: start again today. streak of 1 is better than streak of 0.

'I don't know what to say' was the 3rd most common barrier to prayer (43%). the answer: you don't need fancy words. name 3 things you're grateful for. name 1 thing you did wrong. ask for help with 1 thing. done.

Ramadan is 14 days away. here's a simple prep list: 1. adjust sleep schedule 2. stock up on dates and water 3. set a prayer reminder for each salah 4. tell one friend your goal. that's the whole plan.
41% of prayers happen between 5-7 AM. 33% between 7-9 AM. only 14% after noon. morning prayer dominates because it happens before the world gets loud. protect that window.
free prayer journal template on my gumroad. 30 days of guided prompts. takes 3 minutes per day. no email required. just download and start. link in bio.
8 years of 6-day bro splits. looked the same every year. switched to 3x/week full body. more muscle in 6 months than the previous 3 years. the science backs it up.
the fitness industry says 1g protein per pound of bodyweight. actual research (Morton et al., 2018, 49 studies): 0.73g per pound. for a 185 lb guy that's 135g, not 185g. 3 meals and a shake.
412 guys ran the program. 78% hit all 3 sessions per week. those guys saw 2x the strength gains of the ones who hit 2 out of 3. showing up beats everything.
progressive overload is the entire game. add 2.5 lbs per session when you complete all reps with good form. over 12 weeks: 30+ lbs added to each lift. tiny increments compound.
if you're lifting weights without writing down what you did last session, you're exercising. not training. the notebook is more important than the supplements.
total weekly gym time: 150 minutes. 3 sessions. 50 minutes each. full body compounds. 4 rest days per week. more results than my old 7-8 hour/week bro split.

one case study. ryan. software dev. 34. hadn't trained in 4 years. 12 weeks later: bench +50 lbs, squat +70 lbs, deadlift +80 lbs. lost 17 lbs. waist down 3 inches. tools: the program (free) and a $19 notion tracker.
less than 6 hours of sleep reduces muscle protein synthesis by 18%. no training program overcomes bad sleep. fix your sleep before you buy another supplement.
what 135g protein looks like in a normal day: 3 eggs + yogurt (36g). chicken + rice (42g). salmon + veggies (38g). protein shake (25g). total: 141g. no meal prep army required.
the best program is the one you actually do. if 3x/week is too much, do 2. 2 sessions of full body beats a 6-day split you quit after 3 weeks.
bench went from 135 to 185 in 12 weeks. squat from 155 to 225. deadlift from 185 to 265. total: +200 lbs across all three. same guy. same gym. different program.
walk 10,000 steps today. not for weight loss. for your brain. 30 minutes of walking improves creative output by 60% (Stanford, 2014). the ideas come when your legs move.
training each muscle 2x/week produces more growth than 1x/week at equal volume. that's the meta-analysis. 3-day full body hits everything 2-3x. 6-day split hits everything 1x. math is clear.
4 numbers to track weekly. training volume (sets x reps x weight). bodyweight (weekly average, not daily). waist measurement (same time each week). key lift numbers. that's the whole dashboard.

session A: bench 4x8, OHP 3x10, squat 3x8, dips 3xF, face pulls 3x15. session B: deadlift 3x5, row 4x8, pullups 3xF, curls 3x12, lateral raises 3x15. session C: front squat, RDL, lunges, calves, abs. done.
daily weigh-ins are noise. sodium, water, food. the weekly average is signal. don't react to a single day's reading. ever. plot the weekly averages and watch the trend.
if bodyweight goes up but waist stays flat, you're building muscle. if both go up, you're eating too much. one measurement tells you more than the scale alone.
free 3x/week full body program in my newsletter. the same program 412 guys ran. includes the spreadsheet tracker. link in bio.
creatine is the most studied supplement in sports science. 5g/day. every day. cheap. effective. everything else is marketing until you've maxed out sleep, protein, and training consistency.
tracked my sleep for 365 nights with an Oura ring. the $67 fix that worked: blackout curtains ($15), alarm clock ($10, gets phone out of room), foam earplugs ($2). beat a $3,000 mattress.

alcohol: -38% deep sleep. even 1-2 drinks cut my deep sleep from 1h 10m to 42 minutes. two drinks: 28 minutes. deep sleep is when tissue repair and memory consolidation happen. the wine isn't worth it.
phone charges in the kitchen now. not the nightstand. falling asleep 19 minutes faster. not because of blue light (overblown). because my brain stopped associating bed with scroll time.
every hour my bedtime shifted from my baseline (10:15 PM), sleep quality dropped 12%. including weekends. researchers call it 'social jetlag.' same bedtime every night. hardest fix. biggest payoff.
tested a $2,500 mattress for 90 days against my $800 one. Oura ring sleep scores: zero measurable difference. zero. saved the money. bought blackout curtains instead.
eating within 2 hours of bedtime: -25% deep sleep. body digests when it should recover. last meal by 7:30 PM (bedtime 10:15). simple rule. big impact.
my HRV went from 34ms to 52ms in 12 months. +53%. resting heart rate from 64 to 56 bpm. not from a supplement. from $67 in bedroom changes and consistent behavioral habits.
mouth tape at night. sounds insane. nasal breathing improved my HRV by 8% in 2 weeks. $8 for 90 nights. most underrated sleep hack nobody talks about.
time to fall asleep: month 1 average 28 minutes. month 12 average 9 minutes. -68%. no melatonin. just: phone out of room, consistent bedtime, room at 67F, no food after 7:30.
optimal sleep temperature: 65-68F. your core body temp needs to drop 2-3 degrees to initiate deep sleep. my thermostat was at 72. dropped to 67. immediate improvement. first night.
the $27 tier 1 sleep stack: blackout curtains ($15), alarm clock ($10), foam earplugs ($2). disruptions went from 4 per night to 1. sleep score up 11% from curtains alone.
caffeine has a half-life of 5-6 hours. coffee at 2 PM means half the caffeine is still in your system at 8 PM. cut caffeine by noon. not 2 PM. noon.
top 10% of my sleep nights all shared: 10+ min outdoor morning light, no caffeine after 11 AM, 30+ min exercise, bedroom at 66F, in bed by 10:15, no food after 7:30, no alcohol.
melatonin helped me fall asleep faster but didn't improve deep sleep duration. magnesium glycinate (200-400mg) improved both. $12/month. decent evidence for mildly deficient adults.

blue light glasses: minimal impact in my data. weighted blanket: anxiety benefits, not direct sleep quality. white noise machine: neutral. save your money. fix temperature, darkness, and consistency first.
night wakeups went from 3.1 per night to 1.2. foam earplugs + blackout curtains + consistent bedtime. total cost of those three changes: $27.

one fix tonight. pick one: set thermostat to 67. move phone to another room. stop caffeine at noon. move last meal 2 hours earlier. buy blackout curtains. one fix. track for 3 days. then add the next.
total sleep went from 6h 48m to 7h 22m. +34 min per night. that's 207 extra hours of sleep per year. from behavioral changes and $67 in products.

deep sleep: month 1 was 48 minutes. month 12 was 1 hour 18 minutes. +63%. REM sleep: +44%. these aren't supplement results. these are 'go to bed at the same time and put your phone in the kitchen' results.
free sleep optimization checklist on my gumroad. the exact 5 fixes ranked by impact. $67 total cost. takes 20 minutes to implement the first one. link in bio.
'how's your startup going?' me: refreshing stripe dashboard at 3 AM. revenue: $4.72. total sessions today: 847. all of them are me.
linkedin: 'thrilled to announce' twitter: 'just shipped this, probably broken' reddit: 'roast my landing page' the same person on three platforms
stages of building in public. week 1: 'i will document everything.' week 2: 'i will post updates.' week 3: 'i will post when i have updates.' week 12: posts meme about building in public
client: 'can you make the logo bigger?' me, an intellectual: makes the logo bigger. invoice: $500. sometimes the game is just doing what they asked.
'what's your tech stack?' HTML file on a $5 VPS and an unhealthy relationship with my terminal. next question.
solopreneur math: worked 14 hours today. made $23.50. tells friends: 'yeah the business is going really well, lots of growth'
day 1: 'I will build 10 apps this month.' day 3: has 10 notion docs describing 10 apps. has built zero apps.
therapist: 'and how does that make you feel?' me: 'like a microSaaS founder whose free tier users are complaining about missing features'
if i had a dollar for every time someone said 'just build an AI wrapper' i'd have enough to fund the AI wrapper that would make those dollars back
the five stages of deploying to production: 1. confidence 2. deploy 3. everything breaks 4. panic 5. it was a typo in the env variable
'minimum viable product' is doing a lot of heavy lifting in this sentence when your MVP is a google form and a prayer
what clients say: 'something clean and modern' what clients mean: 'make it look like apple.com but my budget is $200 and i need it by thursday'
people who say 'email is dead' have never seen a cold email sequence close $3,000 in a month. email is alive. your emails are dead. different problem.
two types of indie hackers: 1. ships 10 projects, 3 fail, 2 break even, 5 make money. 2. plans 10 projects, builds zero, writes a thread about why planning matters
'i automated my entire business' means i wrote a python script that posts to twitter while i manually do everything else. and honestly that's fine.
the freelance pipeline: day 1-7 no clients. day 8-14 no clients. day 15 three clients all with urgent deadlines that are somehow all due on the same day
calling yourself a 'solopreneur' instead of 'unemployed but optimistic' is the best rebrand in human history
productivity tip: open 47 tabs. close 46 of them. feel accomplished. open 47 new tabs. this is the cycle and there is no escape.
the real AI disruption isn't replacing jobs. it's giving one person the output of a 5-person team and the imposter syndrome of pretending they did it manually.
which hill are you dying on? i'll go first: a $7 notion template that saves someone 10 hours should cost $29 and i was a coward for pricing it at $7
